document.title: Guidance on Cybersecurity for medical devices
standards: EN ISO/IEC 27000 Information technology — Security techniques — Information security management systems (ISMS) — Overview and vocabulary
EN ISO/IEC 27001 Information Technology – Security techniques – Information Security management Systems – Requirements
IEC 82304-1 Health Software Part 1: General requirements for Product Safety
ISO/IEC 80001-1 Application of Risk Management for IT networks Incorporating Medical Devices

ISO/IEC 80001-5-1 Application of Risk Management for IT networks incorporating medical device – Safety, effectiveness and security in the implementation and use of connected medical devices or connected health software – Part 5-1: Activities in the product life-cycle

IEC/TR 80001-2-2 Application of Risk Management for IT networks Incorporating Medical Devices Part 2-2: Guidance for the Disclosure and Communication of Medical Device Security Needs, Risks and Controls

IEC/TR 80001-2-8 Application of risk management for IT-networks incorporating medical devices – Part 2-8: Application guidance – Guidance on standards for establishing the security capabilities identified in IEC TR 80001-2-2
ISO/IEC 80001-xx including IEC/TR 80001-2-1, IEC/TR 80001-2-3, IEC/TR 80001-2-4, IEC/TR 80001-2-5, ISO/TR 80001-2-6, ISO/TR 80001-2-7 or other
IEC 62443-4-2 Security for industrial automation and control systems. Part 4-2: Technical security requirements for IACS components.
IEC 62443-4-1 Security for industrial automation and control systems. Part 4-1: Secure product development lifecycle requirements.
IEC/TR 60601-4-5 Medical Electrical Equipment – Part 4-5. Safety related technical security specifications for medical devices.

sourceSummary.oneLine: MDCG 2019-16 Rev.1 provides cybersecurity guidance for medical devices under the MDR and IVDR.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Provides manufacturers guidance on fulfilling relevant essential requirements of Annex I to the MDR and IVDR with regard to cybersecurity; the document states it is not legally binding and is not a European Commission document.
keyPoints: Cybersecurity requirements in Annex I of the Medical Devices Regulations address both pre-market and post-market aspects.
Manufacturers should consider state of the art when designing, developing and upgrading medical devices across their life cycle.
Security risk management has the same elements as safety risk management and should be documented in a security risk management plan.
Manufacturers shall set out minimum requirements concerning hardware, IT network characteristics and IT security measures necessary to run software as intended.
Post-market cybersecurity surveillance should include device operation in the intended environment, information sharing, vulnerability remediation and incident response.

entities.name: Medical Device Coordination Group (MDCG)
entities.evidence: This document has been endorsed by the Medical Device Coordination Group (MDCG) established by Article 103 of Regulation (EU) 2017/745.

entities.name: European Commission
entities.evidence: The MDCG is composed of representatives of all Member States and it is chaired by a representative of the European Commission.

entities.name: Court of Justice of the European Union
entities.evidence: Only the Court of Justice of the European Union can give binding interpretations of Union law.

entities.name: Regulation (EU) 2017/745 (MDR)
entities.evidence: The two new Regulations on medical devices 745/2017 (MDR) and 746/2017 (IVDR) have been adopted and entered into force on 25 May 2017.

entities.name: Regulation (EU) 2017/746 (IVDR)
entities.evidence: The two new Regulations on medical devices 745/2017 (MDR) and 746/2017 (IVDR) have been adopted and entered into force on 25 May 2017.

entities.name: ENISA
entities.evidence: ENISA defines Information Security Domain as protection against the threat of theft, deletion or alteration of stored or transmitted data within a cyber system.

entities.name: IMDRF
entities.evidence: The purpose of this work item is to promote a globally harmonized approach to medical device cybersecurity.

entities.name: GDPR
entities.evidence: The General Data Protection Regulation regulates the processing by an individual, a company or an organisation of personal data relating to individuals in the EU.

entities.name: NIS Directive
entities.evidence: The NIS Directive provides legal measures to boost the overall level of cybersecurity in the EU.

entities.name: EU Cybersecurity Act
entities.evidence: The EU Cybersecurity Act introduces for the first time an EU-wide cybersecurity certification framework for ICT products, services and processes.

concepts.name: Cybersecurity requirements in MDR and IVDR Annex I
concepts.pageTarget: Cybersecurity Requirements for Medical Devices in MDR and IVDR Annex I
concepts.evidence: Cybersecurity requirements listed in Annex I of the Medical Devices Regulations, deal with both pre-market and post-market aspects.

concepts.name: Secure by design
concepts.pageTarget: Secure by Design for Medical Devices
concepts.evidence: Secure by design practices in this document contribute to a defence in depth strategy for the product.

concepts.name: Security risk management
concepts.evidence: The process elements are security risk analysis, security risk evaluation, security risk control, evaluation of residual security risk and reporting.

concepts.name: Minimum IT requirements
concepts.pageTarget: Minimum IT Requirements for Medical Device Software
concepts.evidence: Manufacturers shall set out minimum requirements concerning hardware, IT networks characteristics and IT security measures, including protection against unauthorised access.

concepts.name: Security verification and validation
concepts.pageTarget: Cybersecurity Verification and Validation for Medical Devices
concepts.evidence: The primary means of security verification and validation is testing.

concepts.name: Post-market cybersecurity surveillance
concepts.pageTarget: Post-Market Cybersecurity Surveillance for Medical Devices
concepts.evidence: An effective and successful post-market cybersecurity surveillance program should include operation of the device in the intended environment, sharing and dissemination, vulnerability remediation, and incident response.

concepts.name: Vigilance and cybersecurity incidents
concepts.evidence: The manufacturer is responsible for reporting all serious incidents and field safety corrective actions to the CA in accordance with MDR Article 87 or IVDR art 82.

concepts.name: Medical Device Security Disclosure Statement (MDS2)
concepts.pageTarget: MDS2 Medical Device Security Disclosure Statement
concepts.evidence: The MDS2 is an industry-wide and globally accepted form, which can be used to provide the abovementioned security information.

concepts.name: Joint responsibility
concepts.pageTarget: Joint Responsibility for Medical Device Cybersecurity
concepts.evidence: All of these actors share responsibilities for ensuring a secured environment for the benefit of patients’ safety.

evidence.claim: MDCG 2019-16 Rev.1 is titled Guidance on Cybersecurity for medical devices.
evidence.evidenceText: MDCG 2019-16 Rev.1 Guidance on Cybersecurity for medical devices
evidence.pageTarget: MDCG 2019-16 Guidance on Cybersecurity for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: The document was issued in December 2019 and revised in July 2020 as rev.1.
evidence.evidenceText: December 2019 July 2020 rev.1
evidence.pageTarget: MDCG 2019-16 Guidance on Cybersecurity for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: The document was endorsed by the MDCG established by Article 103 of Regulation (EU) 2017/745.
evidence.evidenceText: This document has been endorsed by the Medical Device Coordination Group (MDCG) established by Article 103 of Regulation (EU) 2017/745.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Coordination Group

evidence.claim: The document is not a European Commission document and is not legally binding.
evidence.evidenceText: The document is not a European Commission document and it cannot be regarded as reflecting the official position of the European Commission. Any views expressed in this document are not legally binding.
evidence.pageTarget: MDCG Guidance Legal Status

evidence.claim: The guidance's primary purpose is to help manufacturers fulfil Annex I cybersecurity requirements under the MDR and IVDR.
evidence.evidenceText: The primary purpose of this document is to provide manufacturers with guidance on how to fulfil all the relevant essential requirements of Annex I to the MDR and IVDR with regard to cybersecurity.
evidence.pageTarget: Cybersecurity Requirements for Medical Devices in MDR and IVDR Annex I

evidence.claim: MDR and IVDR require manufacturers to account for risk management, information security, IT security measures, and protection against unauthorised access.
evidence.evidenceText: They require manufacturers to develop and manufacture their products in accordance with the state of the art taking into account the principles of risk management, including information security, as well as to set out minimum requirements concerning IT security measures, including protection against unauthorised access.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Cybersecurity Requirements

evidence.claim: MDR and IVDR Annex I cybersecurity requirements address pre-market and post-market aspects.
evidence.evidenceText: Cybersecurity requirements listed in Annex I of the Medical Devices Regulations, deal with both pre-market and post-market aspects.
evidence.pageTarget: Cybersecurity Requirements for Medical Devices in MDR and IVDR Annex I

evidence.claim: Medical device manufacturers should consider state of the art across the device life cycle.
evidence.evidenceText: The Medical Devices Regulations request manufacturers of medical devices to consider the state of the art when designing, developing and upgrading medical devices across their life cycle.
evidence.pageTarget: State of the Art in Medical Device Cybersecurity

evidence.claim: Risk under MDR and IVDR means the combination of probability of harm and severity of harm.
evidence.evidenceText: ‘risk’ means the combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Risk Definition

evidence.claim: IT security includes confidentiality, integrity and availability for medical devices.
evidence.evidenceText: Key concepts involved in IT security specifically for medical devices are the following: Confidentiality of information at rest and in transit; Integrity; Availability of the processes, devices, data, and connected systems.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device IT Security

evidence.claim: Information security is explicitly required for software-containing devices and software devices under MDR Annex I 17.2 and IVDR Annex I 16.2.
evidence.evidenceText: Annex I section 17.2 (MDR) or 16.2 (IVDR) explicitly sets out that devices which incorporate software or software that are devices in themselves, the software shall be developed and manufactured in accordance with the state of the art taking into account the principles of development life cycle, risk management, including information security, verification and validation.
evidence.pageTarget: Information Security for Medical Device Software

evidence.claim: Security issues should be included in medical device risk assessment where they may have safety impacts.
evidence.evidenceText: When assessing risks in accordance with Annex I of the Medical Devices Regulations, it is important to include security issues in the risk assessment.
evidence.pageTarget: Security Safety Relationship in Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Medical devices should be designed with a layered defence in depth approach and should not rely on operating environment security controls.
evidence.evidenceText: A medical device should be designed in a layered defence in depth approach and therefore should not rely on security controls in the operating environment.
evidence.pageTarget: Defence in Depth for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Exploitable software vulnerabilities may be treated as enablers for reasonably foreseeable misuse.
evidence.evidenceText: Any vulnerability which is deemed to be exploitable for a given implementation of software, might be discovered and exploited over time and as such should be regarded as an enabler for reasonably foreseeable misuse.
evidence.pageTarget: Reasonably Foreseeable Misuse in Medical Device Cybersecurity

evidence.claim: Operators should maintain cybersecurity best practices such as physical security, access control, network segmentation, patch management, malware protection and security awareness training.
evidence.evidenceText: Healthcare providers should adopt a risk management process adhering to general cybersecurity best practices... Good physical security... Access control measures... Network access controls... General patch management... Malware protection... Security awareness training.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Operating Environment Cybersecurity

evidence.claim: Cybersecurity for secured healthcare services is a joint responsibility across manufacturers, suppliers, healthcare providers, patients, integrators, operators and regulators.
evidence.evidenceText: It is important to recognise the roles and expectations of all stakeholders, such as manufacturers, suppliers, healthcare providers, patients, integrators, operators and regulators.
evidence.pageTarget: Joint Responsibility for Medical Device Cybersecurity

evidence.claim: Invalidated modification of a medical device or system can adversely affect performance or safety.
evidence.evidenceText: Any invalidated modification of a medical device or system... can adversely affect system performance or safety in unpredictable ways.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Cybersecurity Change Control

evidence.claim: Security management, specification of security requirements, secure by design, secure implementation, verification and validation, issue management, update management and security guidelines are identified secure product lifecycle practices.
evidence.evidenceText: The following common eight practices define most of the necessary processes... Security management; Specification of security requirements; Secure by design; Secure implementation; Security verification and validation testing; Management of security-related issues; Security update management; Security guidelines.
evidence.pageTarget: Secure Product Lifecycle for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Security risk management elements include analysis, evaluation, control, residual risk evaluation and reporting.
evidence.evidenceText: The process elements are security risk analysis, security risk evaluation, security risk control, evaluation of residual security risk and reporting.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Security Risk Management

evidence.claim: Security risks or control measures that may impact safety and effectiveness should be included in the safety risk assessment.
evidence.evidenceText: When a security risk or control measure could have a possible impact on safety and effectiveness, then it should be included in the safety risk assessment.
evidence.pageTarget: Security Safety Relationship in Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Security capabilities may include authentication, authorisation, encryption, auditing and other security capabilities.
evidence.evidenceText: Security capabilities can include such items as authentication, authorisation, encryption, auditing and other security capabilities a product needs to include.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Security Capabilities

evidence.claim: Threat modelling is described as a systematic approach for analysing security from a hypothetical attacker’s point of view.
evidence.evidenceText: Threat Modelling techniques are a systematic approach for analysing the security of an item in a structural way such that vulnerabilities can be identified, enumerated, and prioritised, all from a hypothetical attacker’s point of view.
evidence.pageTarget: Threat Modelling for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Benefit-risk analysis is required by MDR and IVDR Annex I sections 1, 2, 3e and 8.
evidence.evidenceText: Carrying out a Benefit Risk Analysis is an explicit requirement of the Medical Devices Regulations Annex I, sections 1, 2, 3e and 8.
evidence.pageTarget: Benefit Risk Analysis for Medical Device Cybersecurity

evidence.claim: Manufacturers must provide minimum IT requirements for hardware, IT networks and IT security measures necessary to run software as intended.
evidence.evidenceText: Manufacturers shall set out minimum requirements concerning hardware, IT networks characteristics and IT security measures, including protection against unauthorised access, necessary to run the software as intended.
evidence.pageTarget: Minimum IT Requirements for Medical Device Software

evidence.claim: Instructions for use must include minimum requirements concerning hardware, IT networks and IT security measures for devices with electronic programmable systems or software.
evidence.evidenceText: The instructions for use shall contain... minimum requirements concerning hardware, IT networks characteristics and IT security measures, including protection against unauthorised access, necessary to run the software as intended.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Instructions for Use Cybersecurity

evidence.claim: The operating environment is defined as any IT or network asset interacting with the medical device that is not supplied by the manufacturer.
evidence.evidenceText: The operating environment for a medical device is defined as any IT/network asset interacting with the medical device that is not supplied by the medical device manufacturer.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Operating Environment

evidence.claim: Security verification and validation methods can include security feature testing, fuzz testing, vulnerability scanning and penetration testing.
evidence.evidenceText: Methods can include security feature testing, fuzz testing, vulnerability scanning and penetration testing.
evidence.pageTarget: Cybersecurity Verification and Validation for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Manufacturers should gather post-market security information during the device support lifetime.
evidence.evidenceText: During the support lifetime of the device, the manufacturer should put in place a process to gather post-market information with respect to the security of the device.
evidence.pageTarget: Post-Market Cybersecurity Surveillance for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Technical documentation must include conformity information for Annex I general safety and performance requirements, including security requirements.
evidence.evidenceText: Documentation shall contain information for the demonstration of conformity with the general safety and performance requirements set out in Medical Devices Regulations Annex I. These include security requirements.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Technical Documentation Cybersecurity

evidence.claim: Healthcare provider information may include cybersecurity controls, protective device features, backup and restore procedures, infrastructure requirements and network ports.
evidence.evidenceText: Among the information to be provided to healthcare providers... recommended cybersecurity controls... device features that protect critical functionality... backup and restore features... supporting infrastructure requirements... List of network ports and other interfaces.
evidence.pageTarget: Cybersecurity Information for Healthcare Providers

evidence.claim: MDS2 can be used to provide medical device security information.
evidence.evidenceText: The MDS2 is an industry-wide and globally accepted form, which can be used to provide the abovementioned security information.
evidence.pageTarget: MDS2 Medical Device Security Disclosure Statement

evidence.claim: Post-market cybersecurity surveillance should include operation in intended environment, information sharing, vulnerability remediation and incident response.
evidence.evidenceText: An effective and successful post-market cybersecurity surveillance program should include... operation of the device in the intended environment... sharing and dissemination... vulnerability remediation... incident response.
evidence.pageTarget: Post-Market Cybersecurity Surveillance for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Manufacturers are required to maintain a PMS system under MDR Article 83 or IVDR Article 78, and cybersecurity considerations should be part of it.
evidence.evidenceText: The manufacturer is required to put in place a post market surveillance (PMS) system... in accordance with MDR Art. 83 or IVDR Art. 78. Cybersecurity considerations for medical devices should be part of this PMS system.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device PMS Cybersecurity

evidence.claim: Manufacturers must report serious incidents and field safety corrective actions to the competent authority under MDR Article 87 or IVDR Article 82.
evidence.evidenceText: The manufacturer is responsible for reporting all serious incidents and field safety corrective actions (FSCA) to the CA in accordance with MDR Article 87 or IVDR art 82.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Cybersecurity Vigilance Reporting

evidence.claim: The guidance identifies IMDRF codes for cybersecurity-related device problems and root causes, including A110502 and C1007.
evidence.evidenceText: IMDRF codes available to date include: code A110502... Unauthorized Access to Computer System... code C1007... Software Security Vulnerability.
evidence.pageTarget: IMDRF Cybersecurity Incident Codes

evidence.claim: NIS Directive and GDPR are relevant EU legislative acts that may apply in parallel to the Medical Devices Regulations.
evidence.evidenceText: At EU level, the following legislative acts are relevant... and might apply in parallel to the Medical Devices Regulations: NIS Directive; GDPR.
evidence.pageTarget: EU Cybersecurity Legislation for Medical Devices

evidence.claim: The IMDRF Medical Device Cybersecurity Guide aims to promote a globally harmonized approach to medical device cybersecurity.
evidence.evidenceText: The purpose of this work item is to promote a globally harmonized approach to medical device cybersecurity.
evidence.pageTarget: IMDRF Medical Device Cybersecurity Guide

reviewFlags.reason: Source metadata authoritySignal says FDA, but the source text identifies an MDCG guidance document for the European Union and states it is not a European Commission document.
No product model names are present; productModels is empty.
The document states it is not legally binding and only the Court of Justice of the European Union can give binding interpretations of Union law.
Some OCR/text extraction artifacts are present, including misspellings such as 'ithout', 'IDMRF', and fragmented annex table text.
